130003:
Hitori Gotou, nicknamed Bocchi-chan, is a painfully shy high schooler who has spent years teaching herself guitar alone in her room. She's discovered by drummer Nijika Ijichi and drafted into the band Kessoku Band as their missing guitarist, forcing her to perform at a live house in Shimokitazawa. The show follows Bocchi and her bandmates — Nijika, bassist Ryo Yamada, and vocalist Ikuyo Kita — as they practice, fight over set lists, and try to build an audience from scratch.

Bocchi's social anxiety is the engine of most of the comedy, but the show takes the music seriously too — the songs are good and the live performances are a genuine payoff for watching the band struggle to get there.
21519:
Tokyo high schooler Taki Tachibana and rural village girl Mitsuha Miyamizu begin inexplicably swapping bodies — waking up in each other's lives with no warning and no memory of transitions. They start leaving notes and instructions for each other, and over time develop a connection with someone they've never actually met. When the swaps suddenly stop, Taki goes searching for Mitsuha and discovers that the timeline between them isn't what he assumed.

The film is directed by Makoto Shinkai and is set partly in the fictional mountain town of Itomori and partly across Tokyo. The second half reframes the entire first half and the mystery of how and why the swapping happened becomes the emotional center of the story.
120377:
David Martinez is a street kid in Night City whose mother is killed in gang crossfire, leaving him with nothing but a piece of military-grade cyberware she was transporting. He installs it and falls in with a crew of mercenaries called edgerunners, eventually becoming one himself. The show follows David's rise through the city's mercenary underground alongside netrunner Lucy, while the cyberware he keeps adding to his body slowly takes a toll.

Produced by Trigger and set in the Cyberpunk 2077 universe, the series runs ten episodes. Cyberpsychosis — the condition where too many cybernetic implants destabilize a person's mind — is a recurring threat throughout.
150672:
A gynecologist named Goro Amemiya is murdered on the night his patient AI Hoshino, a secretly pregnant idol, gives birth. Both are reincarnated — Goro as AI's newborn son Aquamarine and his teenage patient Sarina as twin sister Ruby — and grow up retaining memories of their past lives. After AI is killed by a stalker, Aqua enters the entertainment industry to track down who was behind it, while Ruby pursues her own career as an idol.

The show deals heavily with the darker side of Japanese idol culture — how performers are pressured to hide relationships, manufacture public personas, and sacrifice privacy for popularity. Aqua's investigation into his father's identity runs alongside Ruby's storyline throughout the series.
129201:
Shinpei Ajiro returns to his hometown island of Hitogashima for the funeral of his childhood friend Ushio Kofune, whose drowning death is ruled an accident. He quickly suspects foul play, and that night discovers the island is being infiltrated by supernatural entities called Shadows — perfect physical copies of islanders that kill the originals and take their place. When Shinpei is killed, he wakes up back at the moment he arrived on the island with all his memories intact, giving him the ability to loop back and try again.

The series follows Shinpei across multiple loops as he gathers information, uncovers the history behind the Shadows, and tries to find a way to save the people on the island before time runs out.
171018:
Momo Ayase believes in ghosts; Ken Takakura, nicknamed Okarun, believes in aliens. They dare each other to visit a location associated with the other's belief — Momo goes to a UFO hotspot and Okarun goes to a haunted building. Both immediately encounter something real. Okarun is abducted by aliens and partially stripped of his body; Momo channels a deceased fighter spirit to drive off a ghost. They escape with new supernatural abilities and end up working together to deal with the paranormal threats that keep finding them.

The show blends action, horror, and comedy as Momo and Okarun face increasingly powerful supernatural and extraterrestrial enemies. A slow-burn romance develops between them across the series.
184951:
Miyu Suzuki is a cheerful, outgoing high school girl who tends to go along with the crowd. Yuusuke Tani is her quiet, blunt classmate who says exactly what he thinks regardless of social context. Miyu starts asking him random personal questions — partly out of curiosity, partly because she can't stop thinking about him — and the two gradually grow closer despite having almost nothing in common on the surface.

The series is a high school romantic comedy focused on the developing relationship between Miyu and Tani, with a supporting cast of friends who have their own overlapping relationships running in the background.
101291:
Sakuta Azusagawa is a high school student who encounters a phenomenon called Adolescence Syndrome — strange, impossible things that happen to teenagers as a result of emotional distress. His first case is Mai Sakurajima, a famous actress who has become completely invisible to everyone around her except Sakuta. They start dating and investigate other instances of the syndrome together as it begins affecting other girls in Sakuta's life.

Each arc of the series focuses on a different girl and a different manifestation of the syndrome — including Sakuta's own little sister. The series has a companion film, Rascal Does Not Dream of a Dreaming Girl, that continues the story.
166794:
Yuki Itose is a deaf college student who gets by through lip-reading, sign language, and a close group of hearing friends. On a crowded train she encounters Itsuomi Nagi, a multilingual young man who helps her communicate with a foreign tourist and then immediately wants to know more about her. The two slowly build a relationship as Itsuomi learns sign language and Yuki becomes more curious about the world outside her existing routines.

Based on a manga by a deaf author, the series is attentive to the specifics of how Yuki navigates sound-dependent situations. The romance develops gradually across the series with an emphasis on communication and the way both characters expand each other's worlds.
21875:
Set six thousand years before No Game, No Life, this film takes place during a catastrophic war between eighteen sentient races that is gradually destroying the world. Riku is a human strategist who devises a plan to end the conflict by manipulating the gods against each other through games. He's joined by Schwi, a deactivated Ex-Machina — one of the machine races — who has been isolated for decades and wants to understand what a human heart is.

The film covers the events that eventually led to the creation of the rules governing the world seen in the main series. It functions as a standalone story but rewards viewers who are already familiar with No Game, No Life.
20872:
In the near future, androids called Giftias are human enough in appearance and personality to live as companions and family members — but they have a fixed lifespan of just under eighty-two thousand hours, roughly nine years, after which their memories and personality degrade unrecoverably. Tsukasa Mizugaki joins the Terminal Service division of SAI Corporation, the team that retrieves Giftias before their time expires. His assigned partner is Isla, a veteran Giftia who is herself approaching the end of her operational lifespan.

The series follows Tsukasa and Isla as they carry out retrievals together and gradually develop feelings for each other, with Isla's own deadline running in the background throughout.
162780:
Utena Hiiragi is a lifelong magical girl fan who gets recruited not as a hero but as a villain — she's drafted by the evil organization Enormeeta to fight against the magical girls she's always admired. During her first battle she discovers she has a sadistic side that only surfaces when she's in the villain role, which conflicts badly with her self-image as a gentle person. The magical girls she's supposed to be fighting turn out to enjoy the battles, which complicates things further.

The show is an ecchi parody of the magical girl genre told from the antagonist's perspective. It contains explicit content and is intended for adult audiences.
